intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

body.intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

draft.title: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

Building your first draft…

data.intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

facade.hypothesis: Users prefer conversational, low-pressure language ('How's your sleep?' vs 'Sleep Duration: 7.2h') when logging health data
facade.label: Casual check-ins
facade.acceptImplies: Dashboard voice should feel like chatting with a friend, not reading a medical chart
facade.rejectImplies: Dashboard should maintain clinical distance and data-focused terminology

evidence.hypothesis: Users prefer conversational, low-pressure language ('How's your sleep?' vs 'Sleep Duration: 7.2h') when logging health data
evidence.implication: Dashboard voice should feel like chatting with a friend, not reading a medical chart

facade.hypothesis: A health dashboard feels more trustworthy and less clinical when it uses a warm, desaturated palette (soft greens, warm grays, dusty blues) rather than bright or cool tones
facade.label: Soft, muted colors
facade.acceptImplies: User gravitates toward approachable, organic, less medical aesthetics; likely values calm over stimulation
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers clarity through contrast and brightness; may want the dashboard to feel more modern/tech-forward or more direct in its visual feedback

facade.hypothesis: Health dashboards feel more approachable and less clinical when UI elements use generous border-radius rather than sharp corners
facade.label: Soft rounded corners
facade.acceptImplies: User prefers a gentler, more human aesthetic; likely to favor curves, softer edges, and inviting forms throughout the interface
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers precision and clarity; may favor clean geometric shapes, straight lines, and a more structured, minimal approach

facade.hypothesis: A persistent left navigation paired with card-based content blocks will feel organized and skimmable for tracking multiple experiments simultaneously
facade.axisTargeted: Layout structure (sidebar vs tabs) + content grouping (cards vs lists)
facade.label: Sidebar with cards
facade.acceptImplies: User values persistent context + visual chunking of distinct experiments; likely favors depth over breadth navigation
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers tab-based or flat layouts; may want denser information density or sequential focus on one experiment at a time

facade.hypothesis: Users need one key metric dominating the screen, but without surrounding data they won't know if it's good or bad
facade.axisTargeted: Dense data (numbers) vs Key metrics (narrative context)
facade.label: Giant number, tiny context
facade.acceptImplies: User values prominent single stats but worries about shallow interpretation—move toward contextual callouts and trend indicators
facade.rejectImplies: User wants numbers embedded in story—move toward sparklines, status language, and comparative framing

facade.hypothesis: For health dashboards, gradual animated transitions between metric values create more trustworthy perception of data change than instant updates
facade.axisTargeted: Animated transitions vs instant value swaps
facade.label: Smooth slide-in numbers
facade.acceptImplies: User values fluid, graceful feedback; responsive to subtle motion cues; likely prefers eased curves over snappy jumps throughout the interface
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers immediate, unambiguous data presentation; motion may feel wasteful or distracting; likely favors crisp, direct interactions and minimal animation overhead

axes.label: Whether user prefers minimal clinical aesthetics vs. warm, human-centered visual design for health data
Whether user wants data-dense dashboard layout vs. progressive disclosure with one metric at a time
axes.poleA: All at once or step by step?

axes.label: Whether user responds to motivational/encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual reporting voice
Whether user wants celebratory, gamified feedback vs. calm, meditative tracking environment
Whether user prefers raw numbers and charts vs. narrative summaries and insights
Whether user wants instant feedback on actions vs. slow, contemplative animations and transitions

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers minimal clinical aesthetics vs. warm, human-centered visual design for health data
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants data-dense dashboard layout vs. progressive disclosure with one metric at a time
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Prism

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user responds to motivational/encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual reporting voice
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Lumen

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants celebratory, gamified feedback vs. calm, meditative tracking environment
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Aura

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers raw numbers and charts vs. narrative summaries and insights
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Facet

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants instant feedback on actions vs. slow, contemplative animations and transitions
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo

facade.hypothesis: Users respond better to encouraging, celebratory feedback (badges, chimes, affirmations) than neutral confirmations when logging health data, making the dashboard feel rewarding rather than clinical
facade.axisTargeted: friendly vs professional—specifically whether voice should include celebration/reward psychology
facade.label: Celebration dings
facade.acceptImplies: Dashboard should use celebratory language and microinteractions (e.g., 'You crushed it!' with a sound) to reinforce positive health behaviors
facade.rejectImplies: Dashboard should keep feedback minimal and neutral to avoid seeming patronizing or trivializing users' actual health challenges

draft.title: Experiment Tracker Dashboard
draft.summary: A personal health dashboard for monitoring long-term experiments with visual progress tracking, experiment cards, and key metrics at a glance.
